commonIssues: Frost-tender: foliage is killed by first frost; in zones colder than 7, lift tubers after frost and store at roughly 0-10 C in slightly damp peat or sand
Tuber rot in cold, wet, or poorly drained soil — avoid overwatering before sprouts emerge
Powdery mildew on foliage; water the soil at the base, not the leaves
Slugs and snails on young shoots; aphids, spider mites, earwigs, caterpillars, and leafhoppers
Viruses and crown gall (discard infected plants)
Tall varieties topple without staking — set stakes at planting time to avoid injuring tubers later
High-nitrogen fertilizer produces leaves at the expense of blooms — use low-nitrogen feed such as 5-10-10
Container plants dry out much faster than in-ground plants and need more frequent watering and feeding

source.citation: Royal Horticultural Society — How to grow dahlias (RHS Growing Guide): warm sheltered sunny spot; regular watering of containers through the season; potassium-rich liquid feed fortnightly early July to early September in pots; ~50 cm wide container for one standard dahlia; keep indoors until temperatures are consistently above 5 C; stake standard and giant types; flowers summer to first frosts; lift and store tubers frost-free by late November; pests include slugs, snails, aphids, earwigs, capsid bugs, powdery mildew, viruses
RHS Growing Guide — fortnightly potassium-rich (tomato-type) liquid feed for container dahlias July-September; 50 cm (20 in) wide pot for one standard dahlia

Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder — Dahlia (group), Kemper Code a445: winter-hardy USDA zones 7-10; full sun, light filtered shade tolerated in hot summer climates; medium, consistent moisture in organically rich well-drained soil; blooms July-September; lift tuberous roots after first frost and store cool in damp peat; problems include viruses, crown gall, root rot, wilts, powdery mildew, aphids, caterpillars, spider mites, leafhoppers; smaller varieties do well in containers
Missouri Botanical Garden Plant Finder — Dahlia (group): bloom July through September; plant tubers in spring; full sun

Penn State Extension — The Dirt on Dahlias: heavy feeders, monthly low-nitrogen fertilizer such as 5-10-10 or 5-20-20; full sun; keep soil moist but not saturated; killed by frost; store tubers at 32-50 F (0-10 C); stake taller varieties; blooms mid-summer to first frost

Utah State University Extension — Growing Dahlias (fact sheet PDF): select a site with at least 6 hours of sun a day; water often enough to prevent the soil from drying out, soaking soil at the base rather than wetting foliage to prevent mildew; top-dress with 5-10-10 after sprouts emerge; plant after danger of spring frost; stake all but dwarf varieties at planting time; blooms July until killed by autumn frost

Confidence note: sources specify 'water often enough to keep soil moist, never dry' rather than an exact interval; baseIntervalDays=3 (about twice weekly, shortened by hotWeatherMultiplier in heat) is a conservative interpretation of USU/RHS/MOBOT guidance for an actively growing dahlia

Confidence note: container liters converted from RHS '50 cm wide pot per standard dahlia' (~45-55 L); recommendedMinLiters=30 reflects compact/bedding varieties RHS rates suitable for pots — exact liter figures are estimates, not published values

Confidence note: heatStressC=35 is an estimate derived from Missouri Botanical Garden's advice that dahlias appreciate light filtered shade in hot summer climates; no source publishes an exact heat-stress threshold. Planting windows by zone are derived from 'plant after last spring frost' (MOBOT/Penn State/USU) mapped to typical zone frost dates
